([/sæks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAKS*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is an American multinational [investment bank](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") and [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") company. Founded in 1869, Goldman Sachs is [headquartered](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") in the [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City") neighborhood of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), with regional offices in many international financial centers.[[1]](#cite_note-AR-1) Goldman Sachs is one of the [largest investment banks](/wiki/List_of_investment_banks "List of investment banks") in the world by revenue[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is ranked 32nd on the [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") list of the largest United States corporations by total revenue.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the [*Forbes* Global 2000](/wiki/Forbes_Global_2000 "Forbes Global 2000") of 2025, Goldman Sachs ranked 20th.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is considered a [systemically important financial institution](/wiki/Systemically_important_financial_institution "Systemically important financial institution") by the [Financial Stability Board](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Board "Financial Stability Board").

**The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc.** ([/sæks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAKS*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is an American multinational [investment bank](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") and [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") company. Founded in 1869, Goldman Sachs is [headquartered](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters") in the [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City") neighborhood of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), with regional offices in many international financial centers.[[1]](#cite_note-AR-1) Goldman Sachs is one of the [largest investment banks](/wiki/List_of_investment_banks "List of investment banks") in the world by revenue[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is ranked 32nd on the [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") list of the largest United States corporations by total revenue.[[3]](#cite_note-3) In the [*Forbes* Global 2000](/wiki/Forbes_Global_2000 "Forbes Global 2000") of 2025, Goldman Sachs ranked 20th.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is considered a [systemically important financial institution](/wiki/Systemically_important_financial_institution "Systemically important financial institution") by the [Financial Stability Board](/wiki/Financial_Stability_Board "Financial Stability Board").

Goldman Sachs offers services in [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") (advisory for [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") and [restructuring](/wiki/Restructuring "Restructuring")), [securities underwriting](/wiki/Securities_underwriting "Securities underwriting"), [prime brokerage](/wiki/Prime_brokerage "Prime brokerage"), [asset management](/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management"), and [wealth management](/wiki/Wealth_management "Wealth management"). It is a [market maker](/wiki/Market_maker "Market maker") for many types of financial products and provides [clearing](/wiki/Clearing_(finance) "Clearing (finance)") and [custodian bank](/wiki/Custodian_bank "Custodian bank") services. It operates [private-equity funds](/wiki/Private-equity_fund "Private-equity fund") and [hedge funds](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund"). It structures complex and tailor-made financial products. It also owns Goldman Sachs Bank USA, a [direct bank](/wiki/Direct_bank "Direct bank"). It trades both on behalf of its clients ([flow trading](/wiki/Flow_trading "Flow trading")) and for its own account ([proprietary trading](/wiki/Proprietary_trading "Proprietary trading")). The company invests in and arranges financing for startups, and in many cases gets additional business as [bookrunner](/wiki/Bookrunner "Bookrunner") when the companies launch [initial public offerings](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Founding and establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goldman_Sachs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and establishment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Goldman_Sachs.svg/250px-Goldman_Sachs.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Goldman_Sachs.svg)

Second Goldman Sachs logo (1999–2020)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Goldman_Sachs_2022_Black.svg/250px-Goldman_Sachs_2022_Black.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Goldman_Sachs_2022_Black.svg)

Third Goldman Sachs logo (2020–2024)

In 1869, Goldman Sachs was founded by [Marcus Goldman](/wiki/Marcus_Goldman "Marcus Goldman") in New York City in a one-room basement office next to a coal chute.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1882, Goldman's son-in-law [Samuel Sachs](/wiki/Samuel_Sachs "Samuel Sachs") joined the firm.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1885, Goldman's son, [Henry Goldman](/wiki/Henry_Goldman "Henry Goldman"), and his son-in-law, Ludwig Dreyfuss, joined the business and the firm adopted its present name, Goldman Sachs & Co.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The company pioneered the use of [commercial paper](/wiki/Commercial_paper "Commercial paper") for entrepreneurs and joined the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") (NYSE) in 1896.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) By 1898, the firm's capital stood at $1.6 million.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) It opened offices in Boston and Chicago in 1900, San Francisco in 1918, and Philadelphia and St. Louis in 1920.[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13)

Goldman Sachs entered the [initial public offering](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") (IPO) market in 1906 when it took [Sears, Roebuck and Company](/wiki/Sears,_Roebuck_and_Company "Sears, Roebuck and Company") public.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) The deal was facilitated by Henry Goldman's personal friendship with [Julius Rosenwald](/wiki/Julius_Rosenwald "Julius Rosenwald"), an owner of Sears.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) Other underwriting work for IPOs followed, including those of [General Cigar Company](/wiki/General_Cigar_Company "General Cigar Company") also in 1906, [F. W. Woolworth Company](/wiki/F._W._Woolworth_Company "F. W. Woolworth Company") in 1912, and [Continental Can](/wiki/Continental_Can "Continental Can").[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) The firm was an innovator at establishing the [price–earnings ratio](/wiki/Price%E2%80%93earnings_ratio "Price–earnings ratio"), instead of [book value](/wiki/Book_value "Book value"), as a method for valuing companies, and was therefore able to raise funds for retailers and companies with few hard assets.[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13)

In 1912, Henry S. Bowers became the first non-member of the founding family to become a partner of the firm and share in its profits.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) In 1917, under growing pressure from the other partners in the firm due to his pro-German stance, Henry Goldman resigned.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) The Sachs family gained full control of the firm until [Waddill Catchings](/wiki/Waddill_Catchings "Waddill Catchings") joined the company in 1918.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) By 1928, Catchings was the Goldman Sachs partner with the single largest stake in the firm.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) In 1919, the company acquired a major interest in [Merck & Co.](/wiki/Merck_%26_Co. "Merck & Co.") and in 1922, it acquired a major interest in [General Foods](/wiki/General_Foods "General Foods").[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13) On December 4, 1928, the firm launched the Goldman Sachs Trading Corp., a [closed-end fund](/wiki/Closed-end_fund "Closed-end fund").[[14]](#cite_note-14) The fund failed during the [Wall Street Crash of 1929](/wiki/Wall_Street_Crash_of_1929 "Wall Street Crash of 1929"), amid accusations that Goldman Sachs had engaged in share price manipulation and [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading").[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12)

### 1930–1979

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goldman_Sachs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1930–1979")]

In 1930, during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the firm ousted Catchings, and [Sidney Weinberg](/wiki/Sidney_Weinberg "Sidney Weinberg") assumed the role of [senior partner](/wiki/Senior_partner "Senior partner"). Weinberg shifted the firm's focus away from trading and toward [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking").[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) His actions helped to restore some of the firm's tarnished reputation. Under Weinberg's leadership, Goldman Sachs was the lead advisor on the $657 million IPO of [Ford Motor Company](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company") in 1956, a major victory at the time, as well as the $350 million [debenture](/wiki/Debenture "Debenture") offering by Sears Roebuck in 1958.[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13) Under Weinberg's leadership, the firm started an investment research division and a [municipal bond](/wiki/Municipal_bond "Municipal bond") department, and it became an early innovator in [risk arbitrage](/wiki/Risk_arbitrage "Risk arbitrage").[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12)

In the 1950s, [Gus Levy](/wiki/Gus_Levy "Gus Levy") joined the firm as a securities trader, where two powers fought for supremacy, one from investment banking and one from securities trading. Levy was a pioneer in [block trading](/wiki/Block_trade "Block trade") and the firm established this trend under his guidance. Due to Weinberg's heavy influence, the firm formed an investment banking division in 1956 in an attempt to shift focus off Weinberg.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12) In 1957, the firm's headquarters were relocated to 20 [Broad Street](/wiki/Broad_Street_(Manhattan) "Broad Street (Manhattan)"), New York City.[[12]](#cite_note-Cohan2012-12)

In 1969, Levy took over Weinberg's role as senior partner and built the firm’s trading franchise once again.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Levy is credited with the firm’s famous philosophy of being "long-term greedy," which implied that as long as money is made over the long term, short-term losses are bearable. At the same time, partners reinvested nearly all of their earnings in the firm.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Weinberg remained a senior partner of the firm and died in July of that year.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Another financial crisis for the firm occurred in 1970, when the [Penn Central Transportation Company](/wiki/Penn_Central_Transportation_Company "Penn Central Transportation Company") went bankrupt with over $80 million (~$497 million in 2024) in [commercial paper](/wiki/Commercial_paper "Commercial paper") outstanding, most of it issued through Goldman Sachs. The bankruptcy was large, and the resulting lawsuits, notably by the [SEC](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission"), threatened the partnership capital, survival, and reputation of the firm.[[18]](#cite_note-cohan_wpost2012-18) It was this bankruptcy that resulted in [credit ratings](/wiki/Credit_rating "Credit rating") for every issuer of commercial paper today by several credit rating services.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Under the direction of partner Stanley R. Miller, the firm opened its first international office in London in 1970 and created a [private wealth management](/wiki/Private_wealth_management "Private wealth management") division along with a [fixed income](/wiki/Fixed_income "Fixed income") division in 1972.[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13)[[20]](#cite_note-20) It pioneered the "[white knight](/wiki/White_knight_(business) "White knight (business)")" strategy in 1974 during its attempts to defend Electric Storage Battery against a [hostile takeover](/wiki/Hostile_takeover "Hostile takeover") bid from International Nickel and [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley").[[21]](#cite_note-21) [John Weinberg](/wiki/John_Weinberg "John Weinberg"), the son of Sidney Weinberg, and [John C. Whitehead](/wiki/John_C._Whitehead "John C. Whitehead") assumed the roles of co-senior partners in 1976, once again emphasizing the co-leadership at the firm. One of their initiatives was the establishment of 14 business principles.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### 1980–1999

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goldman_Sachs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980–1999")]

On November 16, 1981, the firm acquired J. Aron & Company, a [commodities trading](/wiki/Commodities_trading "Commodities trading") firm that merged with the Fixed Income division to become known as Fixed Income, Currencies, and Commodities.[[23]](#cite_note-23) J. Aron was involved in the coffee and gold markets. The former CEO of Goldman Sachs, [Lloyd Blankfein](/wiki/Lloyd_Blankfein "Lloyd Blankfein"), joined the firm as a result of this merger.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 1983, the firm moved into a newly constructed global headquarters at 85 Broad Street and occupied that building until it moved to its current headquarters in 2009.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) In 1985, it underwrote the public offering of the [real estate investment trust](/wiki/Real_estate_investment_trust "Real estate investment trust") that owned [Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center"), then the largest [REIT](/wiki/Real_estate_investment_trust "Real estate investment trust") offering in history.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In accordance with the beginning of the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), the firm also became involved in facilitating the global privatization movement by advising companies that were spinning off from their parent governments.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 1986, the firm formed Goldman Sachs Asset Management which provides investment and advisory services across public and private markets for institutions, financial advisors, and individuals.[[29]](#cite_note-humbled-29) In the same year, the firm also underwrote the IPO of [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), advised [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") on its acquisition of [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation"),[[29]](#cite_note-humbled-29) and joined the [London](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange") and [Tokyo stock exchanges](/wiki/Tokyo_stock_exchange "Tokyo stock exchange"), where its [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions") grew.[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13) During the 1980s, the firm became the first bank to distribute its investment research electronically and created the first public offering of original issue deep-discount [bond](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)").[[29]](#cite_note-humbled-29) In 1988, it helped the [State Bank of India](/wiki/State_Bank_of_India "State Bank of India") obtain a credit rating and issue US$200 million in the US commercial paper market.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

[Robert Rubin](/wiki/Robert_Rubin "Robert Rubin") and [Stephen Friedman](/wiki/Stephen_Friedman_(economist) "Stephen Friedman (economist)") became co-senior partners in 1990 and pledged to focus on globalization of the firm to strengthen the merger & acquisition and trading business lines.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 1990, the firm introduced paperless trading to the New York Stock Exchange.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Rubin left the firm in 1992 to work in the [Presidency of Bill Clinton](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton").[[13]](#cite_note-brief-13) In 1994, the company launched the [Goldman Sachs Commodity Index](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs_Commodity_Index "Goldman Sachs Commodity Index") (GSCI) and opened its first office in China in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing").[[33]](#cite_note-33) That same year, [Jon Corzine](/wiki/Jon_Corzine "Jon Corzine") became CEO, following the retirement of Friedman as senior partner.[[34]](#cite_note-NewYorkTimes1994September14RamirezAnthony-34)

After decades of debate among the partners, Goldman Sachs became a [public company](/wiki/Public_company "Public company") via an IPO in May 1999.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Goldman Sachs sold 12.6% of the firm to the public, and after the IPO, 48.3% of the firm was held by 221 former partners, 21.2% of the firm was held by non-partner employees, and the remaining 17.9% was held by retired Goldman Sachs partners and two long-time investors, [Sumitomo Bank Ltd.](/wiki/Sumitomo_Bank_Ltd. "Sumitomo Bank Ltd.") and Assn, the investing arm of [Kamehameha Schools](/wiki/Kamehameha_Schools "Kamehameha Schools").[[36]](#cite_note-36) The shares were priced at $53 each at listing. After the IPO, [Henry Paulson](/wiki/Henry_Paulson "Henry Paulson") became chairman and chief executive officer, succeeding Jon Corzine.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### 2000–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Goldman_Sachs&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000–present")]

In September 2000, Goldman Sachs purchased Spear, Leeds, & Kellogg, one of the largest specialist firms on the New York Stock Exchange, for $6.3 billion (~$10.9 billion in 2024).[[38]](#cite_note-NewYorkTimes2000September12McGeehanPatrick-38)

In May 2006, Henry Paulson left the firm to serve as [United States Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury"), and [Lloyd Blankfein](/wiki/Lloyd_Blankfein "Lloyd Blankfein") was promoted to chairman and chief executive officer.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

On September 21, 2008, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, the last two major investment banks in the United States, both confirmed that they would become traditional [bank holding companies](/wiki/Bank_holding_company "Bank holding company").[[40]](#cite_note-Reuters2008September21HallJessica-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) The Federal Reserve's approval of their bid to become banks ended the business model of an independent securities firm, 75 years after Congress separated them from deposit-taking lenders, and capped weeks of chaos that sent [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") into bankruptcy and led to the rushed sale of [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch") to [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America").[[42]](#cite_note-WSJ2008September22HilsenrathJon-42) On September 23, 2008, [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") agreed to purchase $5 billion in Goldman Sachs preferred stock, and also received [warrants](/wiki/Warrant_(finance) "Warrant (finance)") to buy another $5 billion in Goldman Sachs [common stock](/wiki/Common_stock "Common stock") within five years.[[43]](#cite_note-5billion-43) The company also raised $5 billion via a public offering of shares at $123 per share.[[43]](#cite_note-5billion-43) Goldman Sachs also received a $10 billion [preferred stock](/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") investment from the [U.S. Treasury](/wiki/U.S._Treasury "U.S. Treasury") in October 2008, as part of the [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program") (TARP).[[44]](#cite_note-44) In June 2009, Goldman Sachs repaid the U.S. Treasury's TARP investment, with 23% interest (in the form of $318 million in [preferred dividend](/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") payments and $1.418 billion in warrant redemptions).[[45]](#cite_note-45) On March 18, 2011, Goldman Sachs received [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") approval to buy back Berkshire Hathaway's preferred stock in Goldman Sachs.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

On November 16, 2009, Goldman Sachs opened its new headquarters at [200 West Street](/wiki/200_West_Street "200 West Street").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

In September 2011, Goldman Sachs announced that it was shutting down Global Alpha Fund LP, its largest hedge fund, which had been housed under Goldman Sachs Asset Management (GSAM).[[48]](#cite_note-WSJ2011September16RappaportLiz-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Global Alpha, which was created in the mid-1990s with $10 million,[[50]](#cite_note-NYT_20110915-50) was once "one of the biggest and best performing hedge funds in the world" with more than $12 billion [assets under management](/wiki/Assets_under_management "Assets under management") (AUM) at its peak in 2007.[[51]](#cite_note-businessinsider_Carney_20110916-51) Global Alpha used [quantitative analysis](/wiki/Quantitative_analysis_(finance) "Quantitative analysis (finance)") and computer-driven models to invest,[[48]](#cite_note-WSJ2011September16RappaportLiz-48) using [high-frequency trading](/wiki/High-frequency_trading "High-frequency trading"). It was founded by [Cliff Asness](/wiki/Cliff_Asness "Cliff Asness") and [Mark Carhart](/wiki/Mark_Carhart "Mark Carhart"), who developed the statistical models on which the trading was based.[[50]](#cite_note-NYT_20110915-50) Global Alpha was described by *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* as a "big, secretive hedge fund"—the "Cadillac of a fleet of alternative investments" that had made millions for Goldman Sachs by 2006.[[52]](#cite_note-WSJ_Smith_20060420-52) By mid-2008, assets under management (AUM) of the fund had declined to $2.5 billion, by June 2011, AUM was less than $1.7 billion, and by September 2011, after suffering losses that year, AUM was approximately $1 billion.[[53]](#cite_note-Reuters2011September16LaCapraLaurenTara-53)

In August 2015, Goldman Sachs agreed to acquire [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric")'s GE Capital Bank online deposit platform, including $8 billion of online deposits and another $8 billion of brokered certificates of deposit.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Marcus_by_Goldman_Sachs_Logo.svg/250px-Marcus_by_Goldman_Sachs_Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Marcus_by_Goldman_Sachs_Logo.svg)

Logo of Marcus by Goldman Sachs

In April 2016, Goldman Sachs launched GS Bank, a [direct bank](/wiki/Direct_bank "Direct bank").[[55]](#cite_note-55) In October 2016, Goldman Sachs Bank USA started offering no-fee [unsecured](/wiki/Unsecured_debt "Unsecured debt") personal loans under the brand Marcus by Goldman Sachs.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In 2018, Goldman Sachs announced that [David Solomon](/wiki/David_M._Solomon "David M. Solomon") would succeed Lloyd Blankfein as chairman and chief executive officer.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Apple_Card.png/250px-Apple_Card.png)](/wiki/File:Apple_Card.png)

Example of physical [Apple Card](/wiki/Apple_Card "Apple Card"), issued by Goldman Sachs Bank USA

In March 2019, [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") announced that it would partner with Goldman Sachs to launch the [Apple Card](/wiki/Apple_Card "Apple Card"), the bank's first credit card offering.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In August 2021, Goldman Sachs announced that it had agreed to acquire [NN Investment Partners](/wiki/NN_Investment_Partners "NN Investment Partners"), which had $335 billion in [assets under management](/wiki/Assets_under_management "Assets under management"), for €1.7 billion from [NN Group](/wiki/NN_Group "NN Group").[[59]](#cite_note-59)

In January 2026, Goldman Sachs announced that it has entered into an agreement to transition the [Apple Card](/wiki/Apple_Card "Apple Card") program to [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") in 2028.[[60]](#cite_note-60)
